title: 控方证人
summary:
Hammerl experiences recurring, transcendental visions characterized by a joyful, ascending melody that carries him to a state of soaring flight. These experiences are followed by a violent, heavy pulling force that drags him back to reality, causing intense physical pain in his limbs and ears, along with a suffocating sense of oppression. To cope with this feeling of being trapped by walls and ceilings, Hammerl has moved to a sparsely furnished room at the top of his house to face the sky, yet he still feels the need for open countryside.

During a secret dinner, Hammerl confesses his fears of insanity to his friend Seldon, a psychiatrist. Hammerl explains that these phenomena began on a winter night following a dinner with Seldon. He describes his visions as evolving; he is developing non-physical senses that allow him to perceive light, color, and sound beyond human capability. He specifically mentions sensing the colors of magnificent wings, though he cannot describe the colors using earthly terms. Hammerl notes that he feels a constant, agonizing struggle between the "wings"—representing spiritual or transcendent states—and his worldly attachments to comfort, luxury, and pleasure, which seem to pull him in the opposite direction.

Seldon suggests several rational explanations, such as hypnosis, nervous instability, or dreams, but also acknowledges that some things remain inexplicable. He advises Hammerl to either leave London for the countryside or seek out a "cripple" whom Hammerl believes possesses supernatural powers. Hammerl refuses both options, stating he cannot afford to lose his luxuries and is afraid of the man. Seldon concludes that Hammerl must simply wait to see what happens next, though Hammerl expresses desperation regarding the internal battle he is fighting.
